topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The bass line hit me like a physical blow, a sickening confirmation of everything I’d been told. It was *my* melody, the one I’d poured sleepless nights into, now being twisted, sanitized, played by some up-and-comer with zero originality. I watched from the shadows of the dimly lit bar, my knuckles white where I gripped my empty glass. Every note they played felt like a deliberate mockery, a public dismantling of my artistry. A slow, cold burn started in my chest, tightening with a focused intensity. I made a mental note of every face in the room, cataloging them all. When the set ended, I didn't speak. I just walked out, the silence of the street amplifying the sharp, unpleasant taste of righteous anger on my tongue.

[story 2]

I stood by the soundboard, my breath catching in my throat as the familiar, aching chords drifted from the stage. It was mine. My masterpiece. And some stranger was wearing it like a cheap costume. A strange, hot pressure built behind my eyes, a restless energy that demanded release. I couldn't just stand there and let it go; that would be too easy. I needed to ensure they understood the true weight of what they were mimicking. Later, backstage, I found the performer and approached them with a smile that didn't quite reach my eyes. "That," I said, my voice dangerously smooth, "is a very familiar piece. A piece I wrote quite recently. You should probably look into proper attribution before you start selling it." The way their composure fractured under my scrutiny was almost satisfying.

[story 3]

The music flooded the room, vibrant and undeniably mine, yet filtered through someone else's clumsy interpretation. A low thrum vibrated up through the floorboards, and a familiar knot tightened in my gut. I watched the guitarist, seeing the exact posture, the subtle hesitation, that I had painstakingly crafted in my own mind. A slow, deliberate tightening began in my jaw. I didn't move toward the stage immediately. Instead, I started talking to the venue manager, my tone deceptively casual. "I’ve been working on some new material lately. Very personal stuff." I let the implication hang in the air, a subtle threat woven into the casual conversation. When the band finally took a break, I made sure my gaze lingered on the lead singer, a silent promise that the next time they played anything that sounded remotely like my work, the consequences would be far more tangible.
